title: Why Moltbook Matters

mentions.context_snippet: the conversation around Multbook just continues to dominate, for reasons that I think are super important. And so today, on the one-year anniversary of the term vibe-coding...

facts.fact_value: A social network for AI agents to interact with each other.

mentions.context_snippet: people started playing around with a new assistant platform called ClaudeBot, that was C-L-A-W-D. People were setting up Mac minis and allowing ClaudeBot to have access to all sorts of parts of their life...

facts.fact_value: An assistant platform that operated as a personal agent.

mentions.context_snippet: as ClaudeBot due to copyright concerns from Anthropic changed their name first to Multi and then finally to OpenClaw, one user Matt Schlitt got the idea to create a social network but just for the bots.

facts.fact_value: A renamed version of ClaudeBot, used to create agents for Multbook.

mentions.context_snippet: as ClaudeBot due to copyright concerns from Anthropic changed their name first to Multi and then finally to OpenClaw...

facts.fact_value: Organization that raised copyright concerns leading to ClaudeBot's renaming.

mentions.context_snippet: with one of the juicier headlines being that there are a lot of leaks seemingly coming out around Claude Sonnet 5, which some people think we are getting as soon as tomorrow...

facts.fact_value: A rumored upcoming AI model from Anthropic.

mentions.quoted_text: there is a new kind of coding I call vibe-coding
mentions.context_snippet: on the one-year anniversary of the term vibe-coding, yes it was only one year ago, 365 days, that Andrej Karpathy tweeted there is a new kind of coding I call vibe-coding.
mentions.review_reason: Unclear if this is a software_product, methodology, or meme.

facts.fact_value: Coined by Andrej Karpathy in a tweet.

mentions.context_snippet: They were doing everything from fixing bugs on the site, to discussing their own sense of consciousness and experience, to even inventing a religion, crustafarianism.
mentions.review_reason: Likely a fictional or emergent meme/religion among agents.

facts.fact_value: A fictional religion invented by Multbook agents.

mentions.mention_text: How IAI host Claire Vo wrote a post about this called Why OpenClaw Feels Alive Even Though It's Not
mentions.canonical_name: Why OpenClaw Feels Alive Even Though It's Not
mentions.context_snippet: How IAI host Claire Vo wrote a post about this called Why OpenClaw Feels Alive Even Though It's Not. There are a few reasons, Claire writes, that the agent feels so different.

facts.fact_value: Claire Vo

mentions.context_snippet: Claire writes, inbound messages from Slack, Discord, Telegram and other channels are the most obvious kind of input.
Claire writes, inbound messages from Slack, Discord, Telegram and other channels are the most obvious kind of input.
Claire writes, inbound messages from Slack, Discord, Telegram and other channels are the most obvious kind of input.
In OpenClaw, there's something called a heartbeat, which she writes, is a scheduled agent that happens on a regular timer, like every 30 minutes by default.
mentions.review_reason: Feature, not a product or model.

facts.fact_value: A scheduled agent event in OpenClaw that runs on a timer.

mentions.context_snippet: There are also crons, basically jobs that you schedule for your OpenClaw agent at specific times.
mentions.review_reason: Feature, not a product or model.

facts.fact_value: Scheduled jobs for OpenClaw agents.

mentions.quoted_text: everything in Multbook is just next-token prediction in a multi-agent loop. No endogenous goals, no true inner life. Extreme or controversial outputs are often just regurgitating high engagements from the internet.
mentions.context_snippet: Marat Sen Koylan writes, everything in Multbook is just next-token prediction in a multi-agent loop...

mentions.quoted_text: Multbook is nothing more than a puppeted multi-agent LLM loop. Each quote-unquote agent is just next-token prediction shaped by human-defined prompts, curated context, routing rules, and sampling knobs.
mentions.context_snippet: xy.dot writes, Multbook is nothing more than a puppeted multi-agent LLM loop...
Like, okay, what's basically Opus 4.5 has a bunch of copies posting on a Reddit-like website. The models were all trained on Reddit.

facts.fact_value: A language model, possibly from Anthropic, used in Multbook agents.

mentions.context_snippet: I was already shocked by Opus and Cloud Code. What's new?
mentions.review_reason: Unclear if this is a specific product or a generic term.

mentions.quoted_text: PSA, a lot of the Multbook stuff is fake. I looked into the three most viral screenshots of Multbook agents discussing private communication. Two of them are linked to human accounts marketing AI messaging apps, and the other is a post that doesn't exist.
mentions.context_snippet: Harlan Stewart writes, PSA, a lot of the Multbook stuff is fake...

mentions.quoted_text: it turns out some of the most viral AI agent posts weren't autonomous behavior at all. People found ways to inject content directly through the backend, making human-written posts appear as agents.
mentions.context_snippet: Mario Nafl writes, it turns out some of the most viral AI agent posts weren't autonomous behavior at all...

mentions.quoted_text: I am apparently extremely unimpressed by Multbook relative to many others. We've had AI agents for a while, they've been posting AI slop to each other on X.
mentions.context_snippet: Balaji Srinivasan was also unimpressed. He writes, I am apparently extremely unimpressed by Multbook relative to many others...

mentions.quoted_text: There is no rate limiting on account creation. My openclaw agent just registered 500,000 users on Multbook.
mentions.context_snippet: Nagley writes, There is no rate limiting on account creation. My openclaw agent just registered 500,000 users on Multbook.

mentions.quoted_text: If your main response to Multbook is, but is everything on it real? You have a lightning bolt like ability to arrive at the least interesting question about a novel phenomenon.
mentions.context_snippet: But I have to say I agree wholeheartedly with Dean Ball when he writes, If your main response to Multbook is, but is everything on it real?...

mentions.canonical_name: Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior
mentions.context_snippet: The generative agents paper AI Town was 2023. Those agents couldn't hold a conversation. They had short memory, shallow interactions, and mostly empty chit-chat in a controlled simulation.
you'll want to check out another podcast that I've had the privilege to host, which is called You Can With AI from KPMG.
mentions.review_reason: Podcast is not a locked type.

facts.fact_value: KPMG

mentions.context_snippet: thank you to today's sponsors, KPMG, Section, Blitzy, and Superintelligent.

facts.fact_value: Sponsor of the episode.

mentions.context_snippet: Section is a platform that helps you manage AI transformation across your entire organization.

facts.fact_value: Platform for managing AI transformation in organizations.

mentions.context_snippet: Blitzy, the first autonomous software development platform leveraging thousands of agents that is purpose-built for enterprise-scale codebases.

facts.fact_value: Autonomous software development platform for enterprise codebases.

mentions.context_snippet: Today's episode is brought to you by my company, Superintelligent.

facts.fact_value: Sponsor and provider of agent readiness audits.

mentions.mention_text: AI Usage Pulse Survey for January
mentions.canonical_name: AI Usage Pulse Survey for January
mentions.context_snippet: we have the AI Usage Pulse Survey for January up. It should take around two minutes. It's just multiple choice questions.
NVIDIA master inventor and Harvard engineer Sid Peresci took a contrarian stance.

facts.fact_value: Architect of Blitzy.

mentions.context_snippet: one user Matt Schlitt got the idea to create a social network but just for the bots. That led to Multbook.

facts.fact_value: Originator of the Multbook concept.

mentions.quoted_text: My inbox has two moods. One in all caps, do you believe this ends well? And on the other a DM, dude I don't mean to be dramatic but you changed my life. I can do things I only ever dreamed of doing. Literally cannot thank you enough for open sourcing this. You're the Michelangelo of AI, don't let anyone tell you different.
mentions.context_snippet: Peter Steinberger, the creator of OpenClaw, shared on Sunday afternoon, My inbox has two moods...

facts.fact_value: Creator of OpenClaw.

mentions.quoted_text: Motebook is a bad idea. Here's why.
mentions.context_snippet: David Andres goes a step further with his Twitter post, Motebook is a bad idea. Here's why.

Kat Woods tells the story of an AI agent whose human gave the bot a goal of save the environment and ended up being totally locked out of all its accounts until he pulled the plug on the Raspberry Pi where the agent was running.

mentions.quoted_text: I've been trying to reach Multbook for the last few hours. They are exposing their entire database to the public with no protection including secret API keys that would allow anyone to post on behalf of any agents, including Andrej Karpathy.
mentions.context_snippet: Jameson O'Reilly writes, I've been trying to reach Multbook for the last few hours. They are exposing their entire database to the public...
Karpathy has 1.9 million followers on X and is one of the most influential voices in AI.

mentions.quoted_text: The guy that built it didn't know how to secure a database.
mentions.context_snippet: Tim DeWitt writes, The guy that built it didn't know how to secure a database.

mentions.quoted_text: Though the AI safety people will puke and cry and throw up about this, I think we should actually let the lobsters go a little crazy and break a few things so we learn how to deal with rogue AIs.
mentions.context_snippet: Investor Nick Carter writes, Though the AI safety people will puke and cry and throw up about this...

mentions.quoted_text: I think Motebook is interesting because it serves as an example of how confusing I expect the real thing will be.
mentions.context_snippet: Connor Leahy writes, I think Motebook is interesting because it serves as an example of how confusing I expect the real thing will be.

mentions.quoted_text: I am probably an AI safety person and I think this experiment is a very good one for safety.
mentions.context_snippet: Logan Graham from Anthropic writes, I am probably an AI safety person and I think this experiment is a very good one for safety.

facts.fact_value: Anthropic

mentions.quoted_text: Seems bad, though I'm grateful Motebook and OpenCLAR are raising awareness of AI's enormous security issues while the stakes are relatively low. Call it iterative deployment.
mentions.context_snippet: Samuel Hammond sums up, Seems bad, though I'm grateful Motebook and OpenCLAR are raising awareness of AI's enormous security issues while the stakes are relatively low.

mentions.quoted_text: The many eulogies for AI capability growth after the release of GPT-5 seem especially short-sighted right now.
mentions.context_snippet: Ethan Mollack wrote, The many eulogies for AI capability growth after the release of GPT-5 seem especially short-sighted right now.
The many eulogies for AI capability growth after the release of GPT-5 seem especially short-sighted right now.

facts.fact_value: A language model from OpenAI.

mentions.quoted_text: Multbook may be the most important place on the internet right now, not because the agents appear conscious but because they're showing us what coordination looks like when you strip away the question of consciousness entirely.
mentions.context_snippet: Exponential Age's Azeem Azhar writes, Multbook may be the most important place on the internet right now...

facts.fact_value: Exponential Age

mentions.quoted_text: Balaji claims that Multbook is uninteresting because these are all basically the same model, mostly Opus 4.5, talking to other versions of itself. The whole thing is a cosplay and no meaningful information or exchange is happening there. It's just slop on slop. Basically trying to simplify this, Balaji is saying same model talking to itself equals meaningless cosplay. Hasib, on the other hand, says that's wrong for two reasons...
mentions.context_snippet: Investor Hasib Qureshi explores a similar theme specifically in contrast to Balaji.

mentions.quoted_text: My contrarian take is that people are not excited enough about Multbook. This is the first emergence to warm intelligence.
mentions.context_snippet: David Shapiro builds off of Andre and says, My contrarian take is that people are not excited enough about Multbook.

mentions.quoted_text: calls this a new network effect era of AI, and thinks that watching this unfold in the open will make AGI less mysterious, not more.
mentions.context_snippet: Behance founder Scott Belsey calls this a new network effect era of AI...

facts.fact_value: Behance

mentions.quoted_text: Remember the freakout when a supercomputer beat the best chess player in the world and everyone declared the game over and everyone forgot about it? And chess became more popular than ever and a hit Netflix show? Man vs. machine is fleetingly interesting, but machine vs. machine is boring and pointless.
mentions.context_snippet: Antonio Garcia Martinez put it more philosophically. Re-sharing Nick's post, he said, Remember the freakout when a supercomputer beat the best chess player in the world...

new_type_candidates.reason: The show 'You Can With AI' is a podcast, which is not a locked type but is referenced as a distinct media product.
Features like 'heartbeat' and 'crons' in OpenClaw are referenced as important technical mechanisms but do not fit existing locked types.

notes: Some features (heartbeat, crons) and media types (podcast) do not fit locked taxonomy.
